The Delhi High Court, in an order passed on April 13, 2026, permitted the extraction, cryopreservation, and use of
sperm from an Indian Army soldier in a persistent vegetative state, holding that his prior consent to undergo In Vitro
Fertilisation (IVF) treatment with his wife constituted valid consent under Section 22 of the Assisted Reproductive
Technology (Regulation) Act, 2021, and directing that the wife's consent be treated as valid consent on her
husband's behalf for all further steps of the procedure.
Justice Purushaindra Kumar Kaurav of the Delhi High Court ruled that the authorities shall not disentitle the wife on
the sole ground that the husband's fresh written consent was absent, observing that insisting on express consent
from a person in a condition where it is physically impossible and impracticable to provide it would vitiate the
original consent and render otiose the very purpose of the couple's decision to undergo IVF.
The court affirmed that the right to reproductive autonomy is a fundamental right under the Indian Constitution,
and that the Assisted Reproductive Technology (ART) Act must be interpreted in a manner that furthers, rather than
derogates from, that right, while subjecting the procedure to other statutory compliances and the medical condition
of the husband.

streets of Bombay, urging people to observe a hartal in response to the firing on the rebels.
The crowd raided post offices, dug up tram tracks, set up barricades with boulders and barrels, lit bonfires on
the roads, and torched buses and military vehicles, bringing the city to a standstill.
On 22 February, the popular fraternisation escalated into a broader uprising against colonial rule, with workers,

questionText: Which of the following pairs of accused were denied bail by the Supreme Court of India on 5 January 2026 in the 2020 Northeast Delhi riots “larger conspiracy” case?
passageText:
7
Article 21 and Personal Liberty
The protection under Article 21 is available to “persons”, which includes citizens as well as non-citizens.
The Article operates primarily as a limitation on the State, meaning deprivation of life or personal liberty must have
legal authority and follow the prescribed procedure.
The phrase “procedure established by law” means a deprivation can occur only through a procedure laid down in a
valid law.
Article 21 is enforceable through constitutional remedies, and a person can approach the Supreme Court under
Article 32 or a High Court under Article 226 for its protection.
The Supreme Court has held that the procedure under Article 21 must be fair, just, and reasonable, and not arbitrary
(as developed in constitutional jurisprudence).
Article 21 has been interpreted to include the right to live with human dignity, and not merely a physical or animal
existence (as recognised in Supreme Court jurisprudence).
Article 21 has been interpreted to include procedural safeguards in matters involving arrest, detention, and criminal
justice, alongside protections in Article 22 and related laws.
Article 21 has been judicially expanded to cover several rights treated as part of “life” and “personal liberty,” such as
the right to privacy (recognised as a fundamental right through Supreme Court interpretation).
Article 21 is part of Part III (Fundamental Rights) and is among the most frequently invoked provisions in
constitutional litigation because it applies broadly to State action affecting life and liberty.

questionText: Which of the following best describes India’s global economic position announced on 31 December 2025?
passageText:
37
Understanding National Income Measures
Gross Domestic Product: Gross Domestic Product (GDP) is the single standard indicator used across the globe to
indicate the health of a nation's economy. It measures the monetary value of all "final" goods and services—those that
are bought by the final user—produced in a country in a given period. GDP is the sum of private consumption, gross
investment in the economy, government investment, government spending, and net foreign trade (difference between
exports and imports).
Four Key Engines of GDP Growth
Private Final Consumption Expenditure (PFCE): All the money Indians spent for their private consumption.
Government Final Consumption Expenditure (GFCE): All the money the government spent on its current
consumption, such as salaries.
Gross Fixed Capital Expenditure: All the money spent towards investments to boost the productive capacity of the
economy, including business investments in factories and government infrastructure like roads and bridges.
Net Exports (NX): The net effect of exports (what foreigners spent on Indian goods) and imports (what Indians
spent on foreign goods).
GDP = Private Consumption + Gross Investment + Government Investment +
Government Spending + (Exports – Imports)
Gross Value Added: Gross Value Added (GVA) calculates the same national income from the supply side by adding up
all the value added across different sectors. According to the RBI, the GVA of a sector is defined as the value of output
minus the value of its intermediary inputs. This "value added" is shared among the primary factors of product...

questionText: Which of the following countries joined the eurozone before Bulgaria?
passageText:
45
Institutional Integration
Central Bank Changes: With Bulgaria joining the euro area, the Bulgarian National Bank becomes part of the
Eurosystem. The Governor of the Bulgarian National Bank gains a seat on the Governing Council of the ECB. The
Bulgarian National Bank also becomes a full member of the Single Supervisory Mechanism, having been part of the
close cooperation framework since October 2020. The ECB directly supervises four significant institutions in
Bulgaria and oversees 17 less significant institutions.
Market Integration: Bulgarian counterparties of the Eurosystem are now able to participate in ECB open market
operations. The Bulgarian market has joined the Eurosystem's TARGET services, ensuring free flow of cash,
securities, and collateral across Europe. Assets located in Bulgaria that fulfil necessary requirements have been
added to the euro area's list of eligible collateral.
The conversion rate between the euro and the Bulgarian lev was fixed at 1.95583 lev per 1 euro, corresponding to
the central rate of the lev in the Exchange Rate Mechanism (ERM II), as adopted by the EU Council on 8 July 2025.
Bulgaria can exchange lev banknotes and coins for euro free of charge at the Bulgarian National Bank indefinitely,
while commercial banks and post offices offer free exchange until 30 June 2026.
By 2 January 2026, the Association of Bulgarian Banks stated that over 1 billion leva in cash had been converted to
euros through bank deposits, boosting economic transparency as previously untaxed savings entered the monitored
financial circuit.

India - Mercosur Relations
India signed a Framework Agreement with Mercosur on 17
June 2003 in Asunción, Paraguay, with the aim of creating
conditions and mechanisms for negotiations by granting
reciprocal tariff preferences in the first stage and negotiating
a free trade area in the second stage.
As a follow-up to the Framework Agreement, a Preferential
Trade Agreement (PTA) between India and Mercosur was
signed in New Delhi on 25 January 2004, and five annexes to
this Agreement were signed and incorporated on 19 March
2005.
The India-Mercosur PTA became operational with effect from
1 June 2009, implemented in accordance with World Trade
Organization (WTO) rules and providing the framework for
preferential trade between India and the South American
bloc.
A PTA allows countries to give preferential tariff access on certain products, unlike FTAs which are broader and
cover more goods, making the India-Mercosur arrangement more limited in scope than comprehensive free trade
agreements.
The India-Mercosur PTA is implemented through five annexes covering tariff concessions for Indian and Mercosur
products (Annexes I and II), rules of origin (Annex III), preferential safeguard measures (Annex IV), and a dispute
settlement mechanism (Annex V).
By this PTA, India and Mercosur have ...

Lokayuktas in India
The Lokayukta is a statutory anti-corruption ombudsman established at the state
level in India, designed to investigate and address complaints of corruption and
maladministration against public servants, including politicians, bureaucrats, and
other government officials. The institution aims to promote transparency and
accountability within state administrations.
Historical Background
1966: The concept of the Lokayukta was first recommended by the Administrative
Reforms Commission (ARC) led by Morarji Desai. The ARC's interim report
emphasized the need for an independent authority at the state level to address
grievances against public administration.
1968: Based on the ARC's recommendations, the Government of India introduced a
bill proposing the establishment of the Lokpal at the central level and Lokayuktas
in the states. Although the bill did not pass at that time, it laid the foundation for
states to enact their own legislation.
188
1971: Maharashtra became the first state to establish the Lokayukta institution by enacting The
Maharashtra Lokayukta and Upa-Lokayuktas Act, which came into effect on October 25, 1972.
Subsequent Developments: Following Maharashtra's lead, several other states enacted their own
Lokayukta Acts, including Odisha (1971), Rajasthan (1973), Bihar (1974), Uttar Pradesh (1975), and
Karnataka (1984). Each state's legislation varies, leading to differences in the powers and functions of the
Lokayukta across states.
The Lokpal and Lokayuktas Act, 2013 is a significant legislative measure aimed at combating corruption in India.

questionText: How and which legislative body first passed the Transgender Persons (Protection of Rights) Amendment Bill, 2026?
passageText:

Enhanced Penal Provisions Under the 2026 Bill
The bill retains the existing offences under the 2019 Act—including forced or bonded labour, denial of public access,
and physical or psychological harm—and adds four new categories of enhanced offences with substantially
increased punishments.
For the offence of kidnapping an adult with grievous hurt or permanent injury to compel assumption of a
transgender identity, the bill prescribes a minimum of ten years of rigorous imprisonment (RI), extendable to life
imprisonment, along with a minimum fine of Rs 2 lakh; the same offence committed against a child mandates life
imprisonment and a minimum fine of Rs 5 lakh.
For forcing an adult to present as a transgender person and engage in begging, servitude, or bonded labour, the bill
stipulates five to ten years of RI along with a minimum fine of Rs 1 lakh; the same offence against a child attracts ten
to fourteen years of RI and a minimum fine of Rs 3 lakh.
Concerns Raised Against the 2026 Amendment
Critics, including transgender activists and community organisations, contend that the removal of Section 4(2) of
the 2019 Act directly undermines the foundational principle of the NALSA judgment, which had held that gender
self-determination is an individual right that does not require external validation, particularly through medical
examination.
The requirement of a Medical Board recommendation introduces clinical gatekeeping into a process that was
previously administrative, effectively treating transgender identity as a medical condition rather than a personal or
social identity; ...

questionText: subsidy dispute was agreed at which WTO Ministerial Conference?
passageText:
55
The agreement is open for accession to any WTO member at any time, and its support base of 128 countries
representing three-quarters of the WTO's total membership reflects its broad appeal particularly amongdeveloping
economies and LDCs. 
India's Objections to the IFD Agreement
India's foundational objection is that investment facilitation constitutes a non-mandated, non-multilateral issue: the
WTO was established to regulate trade in goods, services, and intellectual property, and expanding its mandate to
govern FDI without a unanimous multilateral decision violates the spirit and text of the Marrakesh Agreement.
India views the plurilateral route being used to advance the IFD as a structural threat to the WTO's consensus-based,
member-driven character, arguing that allowing groups of willing members to move ahead on specific issues
without the agreement of the full membership effectively creates a two-tier WTO dominated by major economies, in
which issues of particular importance to developing countries and LDCs can be bypassed.
New Delhi has warned that if plurilateral agreements are normalised within the WTO framework, powerful

Quarterly GDP Estimation Methodology
The NSO under MoSPI calculates quarterly GDP estimates using the Benchmark-Indicator methodology a globally
standard approach following SNA 2008 and the IMF’s Quarterly National Accounts Manual 2017 wherein annual
GDP estimates serve as the benchmark and high-frequency monthly or quarterly indicators are applied to
extrapolate estimates for individual quarters.
High-frequency indicators used in the new quarterly GDP series include the Cement Production Index, Finished Steel
Consumption, Index of Industrial Production (IIP) for various sub-sectors, Central GST (CGST) collections, railway
freight and passenger data, cargo handled at major and minor ports, domestic and international air passenger traffic,
and data on commercial vehicle sales and household vehicle registrations.
Related Aggregates and Institutional Concepts
Net National Income is derived from GDP by subtracting Capital Consumption (depreciation, denoted as ‘d’) and
adding Net Factor Income from Abroad (NFIA): NNI = GDP − d + NFIA; Gross National Income (GNI) equals GDP plus
Net Primary Income from the Rest of the World; Gross National Disposable Income (GNDI) equals GNI plus other
current transfers (net) from the rest of the world.
The base year is the reference year whose prices are used to calculate real GDP growth over time; rebasing is the
process of updating this reference year using improved data and methods to better reflect the current structure of
the economy, with the new base year becoming the reference point for estimating GDP and key indices including
Consume...

The alignment of the Line of Actual Control (LAC) in the eastern sector broadly follows the 1914 McMahon Line; the
LAC is the operative demarcation that separates Indian-controlled territory from Chinese-controlled territory in the
absence of a formally delimited and ratified boundary.
The Radcliffe Line
The Radcliffe Line is the boundary that divided British
India into the independent nations of India and Pakistan
at the time of Partition in 1947, named after Sir Cyril
Radcliffe, who chaired the Boundary Commissions
responsible for drawing the line under severe time
pressure.
The Radcliffe Line was drawn along two axes: the
western side separated West Pakistan (now Pakistan)
from India, and the eastern side separated India from
East Pakistan (now Bangladesh); the western portion of
the line continues to serve as the Indo-Pakistani
international border, while the eastern portion serves
as the India-Bangladesh border.

exclusive legislative competence; key subjects include defence and armed forces, foreign affairs, atomic energy,

uniform foreign policy and economic framework.
The State List (List II) currently contains 61 subjects originally 66 on which state legislatures have exclusive legislative
competence; key subjects include police and public order, public health and sanitation, agriculture, local government,
state taxes, and prisons, with this list designed to strengthen state autonomy and reflect regional priorities and
diversity.
The Concurrent List (List III) currently contains approximately 52 subjects originally 47 on which both Parliament and
state legislatures may legislate, with Central law prevailing in the event of conflict unless the state law has received
Presidential assent under Article 254; key subjects include education, forests and wildlife, marriage and divorce,
criminal law and procedure, labour welfare, and social security.
The Three Lists and Their Significance
Article 248 of the Constitution grants Parliament the exclusive power to legislate on subjects not enumerated in any of
the three lists known as residuary subjects a provision that tilts residual legislative power toward the Centre and
ensures the Union can address new and evolving areas as they emerge.

questionText: Which of the following cosmic events produced the first gravitational wave detected by LIGO in 2015?
passageText:
49
When a gravitational wave passes through, it affects the two arms differently as one arm extends, the other contracts,
and vice versa, in a pattern technically referred to as "Differential Arm" motion or differential displacement; this
causes the light waves from the two arms to travel different distances, preventing cancellation, so that the light
escapes through the splitter and strikes the light detector, registering a signal.
A photodetector tracks the timing and motion of the returning laser pulses; this signal, along with corroborating
signals from other detectors in the global network, is then used to study and characterise the gravitational wave
event and trace its cosmic origin.
The measurement requires extraordinary precision as the physical effects of gravitational waves are extremely small
typically causing fractional length changes of approximately 10⁻²¹ over kilometre-scale distances, making LIGO-class
instruments among the most sensitive measurement devices ever constructed.
Current Construction Status and Procurement Timeline
The Centre's financial approval of ₹2,300 crore for LIGO-India was granted in April 2023; the EPC tender worth
₹1,600 crore was floated approximately two years later in April 2025, with construction originally expected to
commence in the second half of 2025 according to statements made by Sunil Ganju, the then Scientific Secretary of
DAE, at the time of the tender's issuance.
Since April 2025, the tender has undergone multiple date extensions, as recorded on the Government eMarketplace
(GeM) the Central public procurement portal; as ...

questionText: In which case did the Supreme Court on 11 March 2026 permit withdrawal of life-sustaining treatment for a specific patient under India’s passive euthanasia framework?
passageText:
62
The Supreme Court explained that active euthanasia involves a positive act
intended to directly cause death, such as administering a lethal substance.
Passive euthanasia, by contrast, involves the withdrawal or withholding of
medical treatment that artificially sustains life. The Court emphasized that the
real distinction is not simply “act versus omission,” but “causing death” versus
“allowing death to occur” by removing artificial barriers to the natural course of
an underlying condition.
Passive
Euthanesia
In Harish Rana, the Court made an important clarification: CANH through a PEG tube is medical treatment,
not merely ordinary feeding or basic care. Since it is a technologically mediated, clinically supervised
intervention requiring medical skill and judgment, it can lawfully fall within the passive euthanasia framework if
continuation is medically futile and contrary to the patient’s best interests.
Supreme Court Guidelines on Passive Euthanasia
Original 2018 Directives
The 2018 guidelines required a two-stage medical review process for passive euthanasia cases.
The Primary Medical Board was to be constituted by the hospital, including the Head of the treating department and
at least three specialists from general medicine, cardiology, neurology, nephrology, psychiatry, or oncology, each
with 20 years of experience.
The Secondary Medical Board was to be appointed by the district collector, including the Chief District Medical
Officer as Chairman and three expert doctors from the same fields.
Earlier, it was necessary that a judicial magistrate attest or countersign...

The Regulating Act of 1773 established the first Supreme Court at Calcutta; Supreme Courts were subsequently
established at Bombay in 1800 and Madras in 1823.
The High Courts Act of 1861 created High Courts across British India and simultaneously abolished the existing
Supreme Courts.
The Government of India Act, 1935 established the Federal Court of India, which served as the institutional
predecessor to the present Supreme Court until 1950.
The Constitutional and Legislative Process for Expansion
Under Article 124(1), Parliament holds the sole authority to increase the sanctioned strength of the Supreme Court;
unlike High Courts, where the President can augment judicial strength through executive order under Article 216,
the Supreme Court's strength can only be altered through an Act of Parliament.
The process begins with the CJI sending a formal proposal to the Union Ministry of Law and Justice, followed by
inter-ministerial consultation with the Ministry of Finance on budgetary implications, before Cabinet approval is
sought.
The amendment bill must be passed by both the Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha by a simple majority of members
present and voting, before receiving the President's assent to take legal effect.
Implementation proceeds through the Collegium system: the Supreme Court Collegium, comprising the CJI and the
four most senior judges by length of service, recommends names for appointment, and the President formally makes
appointments on receipt of these recommendations.

questionText: Which of the following is the main deliberative and policy-making body of the United Nations?
passageText:

The UN Security Council: Composition, Powers, and Challenges
Structure and Membership
The UN Security Council (UNSC) is one of the six principal organs of the UN, established in 1945 under
the UN Charter; its first session was held on January 17, 1946 at Church House, Westminster, London,
with its permanent headquarters located in New York City.
The UNSC comprises 15 members: five Permanent Members (P5), namely the United States, the United
Kingdom, Russia, France, and China, and ten Non-Permanent Members elected by the General
Assembly to serve two-year terms, with five elected each year. The Presidency of the Security Council
rotates monthly among all 15 members; India held the Presidency in 2022.
In voting, each member holds one vote; P5 nations possess veto power, meaning a negative vote by any
P5 member defeats a resolution regardless of the votes of other members; member states without UNSC
membership may participate in deliberations without a vote when their interests are directly affected.
Key UNSC Committees and Resolutions
On counterterrorism, the UNSC adopted Resolution 1373, establishing the Counter Terrorism
Committee, and Resolution 1267, establishing the Da'esh and Al Qaeda Committee.
On non-proliferation, Resolution 1540 addresses the threat posed by nuclear, chemical, and biological
weapons falling into non-state hands.
India and the Security Council
India has served as a Non-Permanent Member of the Security Council seven times and was elected for
an eighth term covering 2021-22.
India's arguments for a permanent seat rest on several grounds: participati...
options: Security Council
Secretariat
General Assembly
International Court of Justice
solution:

Principal Features of the Draft Rules Released for Public Consultation
The Ministry of Rural Development released eight draft rules on 23 May 2026, with a one-month window for public

The Eight Draft Rules:
National Level Steering Committee Rules

Central Gramin Rozgar Guarantee Council Rules
Manner of Payment of Wages and Unemployment Allowance Rules
Manner and Procedure of Expenditure Incurred by the State in Excess of the Normative Allocation and
Expenses of the Scheme for UTs without Legislature Rules, 2026
Normative Allocation Framework:
The draft Objective Parameters for Normative Allocation Rules state that the Central Government shall, for
each financial year, determine the normative allocation of funds for every State based on objective
parameters specified in the rules.
The Centre will use the 16th Finance Commission's horizontal devolution recommendations to determine
allocations.
The formula relies heavily on GSDP Distance (42.5% weightage), which measures how far a State's per
capita income falls short of the wealthiest States, ensuring poorer States receive a larger share; population
carries 17.5% weightage, while all other metrics carry 10% weightage each.
The Union government has allocated Rs 95,692.31 crore for the VB-G RAM G scheme for the financial year
2026-27.

questionText: Which of the following persons delivered the 2026 CIC ruling on the RTI status of the BCCI?
passageText:
43
Following IPL spot-fixing controversies, the Supreme Court imposed major governance reforms through the
Lodha Committee recommendations, including tenure limits, conflict-of-interest norms, and the one-state-one-
vote principle.
The Court clarified that even though the BCCI is not "State" under Article 12, it can still be subject to judicial
review under Article 226 (writ jurisdiction of High Courts) because it performs public functions.
However, the Court stopped short of declaring the BCCI a public authority under the RTI Act.
Dalco Engineering v. Satish Prabhakar Padhye (2010):
The Supreme Court clarified that substantial government financing or control is necessary for an entity to fall
within statutory public accountability frameworks.
Law Commission's 275th Report (2018):
The Law Commission of India recommended that sports bodies performing public functions should come under
the RTI framework because they exercise "state-like powers" and the BCCI virtually functions as a National
Sports Federation.
The Report noted that the BCCI had received tax exemptions worth Rs 2,100 crore in the period 1997-2007
alone, which the Commission interpreted as foregone state revenue.
2018 CIC Order:
Then-Commissioner M. Sridhar Acharyulu ruled that the BCCI qualified as a public authority under Section 2(h)
and directed it to appoint PIOs and create an RTI compliance mechanism.
The BCCI challenged the order before the Madras High Court, which stayed it and remanded the matter back to
the CIC for reconsideration in September 2025 in light of Supreme Court rulings.
Earlier Recommendat...
options: M. Sridhar Acharyulu
P. R. Ramesh
R. M. Lodha
Mukul Mudgal
solution:
Step 1: Based on the official CLAT Post documentation for Summits, Treaties & Defense (May 2026), option (C) is the correct response.
Step 2: Question Context: Which of the following persons delivered the 2026 CIC ruling on the RTI status of the BCCI?
Step 3: Correct Answer: (C) R. M. Lodha

questionText: Which of the following persons chaired the Court of Arbitration constituted under the Indus Waters Treaty dispute?
passageText:
50
commissioners from both sides, which is required to meet annually.
The Commission conducts a general inspection of all rivers in parts once every five years; total inspection tours
conducted so far exceed 100. The Commission has held over 100 meetings to date, including those convened to
address Pakistani objections.
Three-Tiered Dispute Resolution Mechanism:
Permanent Indus Commission: Any questions arising between the parties are initially resolved at the Commission
level, or can also be taken up at the inter-government level.
Neutral Expert: If differences of a technical nature remain unresolved, they are referred to a Neutral Expert
appointed by the World Bank.
Court of Arbitration: If the Neutral Expert determines the issue is legal rather than purely technical, or if both
parties agree, a formal "dispute" is referred to a Court of Arbitration set up by the World Bank; this constitutes the
treaty's graded, sequential mechanism.
Hydroelectric Projects Under IWT Inspection:
Pakal Dul and Lower Kalnai: Pakal Dul Hydro Electric Project is built on the river Marusudar, a tributary of the
Chenab; Lower Kalnai is developed on the Chenab.
Kishenganga Hydroelectric Project: A run-of-the-river project located in Jammu and Kashmir on the Kishenganga
River (called the Neelum River in Pakistan).
Ratle Hydroelectric Project: A run-of-the-river hydroelectric power station on the Chenab River in Jammu and
Kashmir.
India's Objection to the Court of Arbitration
The Problem of Parallel Proceedings:
The core of the legal dispute lies in Pakistan's insistence on bypassing the Neutral Ex...
options: Sir Daniel Bethlehem
Syed Haider Shah
Prof. Sean D. Murphy
Prof. Philippa Webb
solution:
Step 1: Based on the official CLAT Post documentation for Summits, Treaties & Defense (May 2026), option (D) is the correct response.

examinations.
Multi-Session and Multi-Stage Testing: For large-scale examinations like NEET-UG, the Committee proposed
conducting examinations across multiple sessions and exploring multi-stage screening models to reduce logistical
pressure and improve fairness.
Secure Standardised Testing Infrastructure: Establishment of at least 1,000 permanent secure testing centres
across the country, mainly in reputed government institutions, equipped for CBT, pen-and-paper testing (PPT), and
hybrid testing modes. Mobile testing centres were proposed for remote and inaccessible regions such as the North-
East, Himalayan states, and island territories to ensure equitable access.

topic: Governor's Power Over State Bills & Constitutional Timeframes
questionText: Under Article 200, can a Governor withhold assent a second time after a Bill is repassed by the State Assembly?
passageText: The constitutional office of the Governor has been central to ongoing federal litigation regarding the legislative process. Under Article 200 of the Constitution, when a Bill passed by the State Legislative Assembly is presented to the Governor, the Governor has four options: grant assent, withhold assent, reserve the Bill for the consideration of the President, or return the Bill (if not a Money Bill) with a message requesting reconsideration. In a landmark clarification, the Supreme Court held that if the Assembly repasses the Bill with or without amendment, the Governor is constitutionally obligated to grant assent and cannot exercise a pocket veto.
options: Yes, unconditionally
No, assent becomes mandatory
Only with Supreme Court permission
Only for Money Bills
solution: The proviso to Article 200 makes assent mandatory once a returned Bill is repassed by the House.
conceptTip: Art 200 = Governor Assent | Art 201 = President Reserve.

topic: Governor's Power Over State Bills & Constitutional Timeframes

options: Article 200

Article 245
solution: Article 201 deals with Bills reserved by the Governor for Presidential consideration.
conceptTip: Art 200 = Governor Assent | Art 201 = President Reserve.

topic: Governor's Power Over State Bills & Constitutional Timeframes
questionText: What happens if a State Legislative Assembly re-passes a returned non-Money Bill with or without amendments?
passageText: The constitutional role of the Governor under Article 200 has emerged as a major flashpoint in Indian federalism. Article 200 provides four distinct courses of action when a Bill passed by the State Legislature is presented to the Governor: granting assent, withholding assent, returning the Bill for reconsideration (if not a Money Bill), or reserving the Bill for the President’s consideration under Article 201. The Constitution uses the phrase 'as soon as possible' for returning a Bill. In recent landmark decisions, the Supreme Court clarified that a Governor cannot withhold assent indefinitely without returning the Bill to the Assembly. If the Assembly passes the Bill again with or without amendment, the Governor is constitutionally bound to grant assent.
options: The Governor must reserve it for the Prime Minister.
The Governor is bound to grant assent under Article 200 proviso.
The Bill automatically lapses.
The Governor can dissolve the Assembly.
solution: Under the proviso to Article 200, if the Assembly re-passes the returned Bill, the Governor shall not withhold assent.
conceptTip: Art 200 = Governor Assent | Proviso = Mandate on Re-passage | Art 201 = President.

topic: Parliamentary Procedure on Finance Bill & Union Budget Passage
questionText: What happens when the Lok Sabha Speaker applies the 'guillotine' during Budget discussions?
passageText: The passage of the Union Budget through Parliament involves a meticulous multi-stage constitutional procedure. Under Article 112, the President causes to be laid before both Houses the 'Annual Financial Statement'. Following general discussion, the Lok Sabha votes on the 'Demands for Grants'. Due to time constraints, on the final allotted day for voting, the Speaker applies the 'guillotine' procedure, putting all remaining undiscussed demands to vote simultaneously. Once demands are passed, the Appropriation Bill is introduced under Article 114 to authorize expenditure from the Consolidated Fund of India. Finally, the Finance Bill containing taxation proposals is passed. Money Bills, defined strictly under Article 110, require Presidential recommendation and Lok Sabha supremacy; Rajya Sabha can only delay a Money Bill for 14 days.
options: All pending bills are automatically referred to the Supreme Court for judicial review.
All remaining undiscussed Demands for Grants are put to vote immediately without further debate.
The House is adjourned sine die for six months.
The Finance Minister must resign immediately.
solution: Guillotine is a parliamentary procedure to conclude voting on all remaining Demands for Grants when time expires.
conceptTip: Art 110 = Money Bill | Art 112 = Budget Statement | Art 114 = Appropriation.

topic: Parliamentary Procedure on Finance Bill & Union Budget Passage
questionText: Within how many days must the Rajya Sabha return a Money Bill to the Lok Sabha?
options: 7 days
14 days
30 days
60 days
solution: Under Article 109(2), Rajya Sabha must return a Money Bill within 14 days, or it is deemed passed.
conceptTip: Art 110 = Money Bill | Art 112 = Budget Statement | Art 114 = Appropriation.

topic: Waqf Law Amendment & Constitutional Property Litigation
questionText: Which constitutional amendment removed the right to property from Fundamental Rights?
passageText: The administration of Waqf properties in India sits at the intersection of religious freedoms, property law, and secular state governance. Under Article 26 of the Constitution, religious denominations enjoy the right to establish and maintain institutions for religious and charitable purposes and manage their own affairs in matters of religion. However, property management remains subject to state regulation under public order, morality, and health. The Waqf Act 1995 established State Waqf Boards and the Central Waqf Council as statutory bodies to oversee dedicated properties. Following the 44th Constitutional Amendment of 1978, the right to property ceased to be a Fundamental Right under Article 31 and became a constitutional right under Article 300A.
options: 42nd Amendment
44th Amendment
86th Amendment
103rd Amendment
solution: The 44th Amendment Act (1978) deleted Article 31 and inserted Article 300A.
conceptTip: Art 26 = Manage Religious Affairs | Art 300A = Property Right.

topic: Waqf Law Amendment & Constitutional Property Litigation
questionText: Which Article of the Constitution guarantees the freedom to manage religious affairs?
options: Article 25
Article 26
Article 29
Article 30
solution: Article 26 guarantees freedom to manage religious affairs.
conceptTip: Art 26 = Manage Religious Affairs | Art 300A = Property Right.

questionText: Which international financial institution mediated the negotiation of the 1960 Indus Waters Treaty?
passageText: The bilateral relationship between India and Pakistan remains heavily dictated by historical conflict, water security treaties, and multilateral counter-terrorism frameworks. The Indus Waters Treaty of 1960, brokered by the World Bank, stands as one of the most durable water-sharing agreements in international law. Under the treaty, the waters of the three Eastern rivers—the Ravi, Beas, and Sutlej—were allocated to India, while the waters of the three Western rivers—the Indus, Jhelum, and Chenab—were allocated to Pakistan. Despite several wars and diplomatic standoffs, the treaty survived. However, modern security dynamics, including cross-border terrorism and climate change impact on Himalayan glaciers, have strained bilateral dialogue. questionText: What is the key jurisdictional distinction between the ICJ and the ICC?
passageText: The legal landscape surrounding the Israeli-Palestinian conflict has entered a crucial phase before international judicial bodies. The International Court of Justice (ICJ) at The Hague has been called upon to examine both provisional measures applications under the 1948 Genocide Convention and advisory requests regarding the legal consequences of prolonged occupation. Under international humanitarian law, particularly the Fourth Geneva Convention of 1949, occupying powers are strictly prohibited from transferring parts of their own civilian population into occupied territory. India has consistently advocated for a negotiated Two-State solution, recognizing Palestine in 1988 while maintaining strong bilateral ties with Israel.
options: ICJ hears criminal cases against individuals; ICC hears state disputes.
ICJ hears disputes between sovereign states; ICC prosecutes individuals.
ICJ is an organ of the EU; ICC is an organ of the UN.
ICJ has no advisory jurisdiction.
solution: ICJ handles inter-state disputes and UN advisory requests; ICC prosecutes individuals for war crimes and genocide.
conceptTip: ICJ = Hague (Peace Palace) | 15 Judges | 9-year terms.

topic: New START Treaty Expiry & Nuclear Arms Control Geopolitics
questionText: Where was the New START Treaty signed in 2010?
passageText: The expiration and suspension of bilateral nuclear arms control treaties between the United States and Russia marks a volatile shift in global strategic stability. The New START (Strategic Arms Reduction Treaty), signed in Prague in 2010, capped deployed strategic nuclear warheads at 1,550 for each nation and established rigorous on-site verification inspections. As the last major bilateral nuclear treaty governing over 80% of the world's nuclear warheads, its demise affects the broader framework of the 1968 Non-Proliferation Treaty (NPT). India, while remaining outside the NPT as a non-signatory, maintains a doctrine of 'No First Use' and credible minimum deterrence.
options: Geneva
Prague
Vienna
Helsinki
solution: New START was signed in Prague, Czech Republic in April 2010.
conceptTip: Prague 2010 = New START | 1,550 Warheads.

topic: New START Treaty Expiry & Nuclear Arms Control Geopolitics

topic: Russia–Ukraine War, NATO Expansion & Strategic Autonomy
questionText: Which country recently joined NATO as its 32nd member state?
passageText: The conflict between Russia and Ukraine has fundamentally reshaped the post-Cold War security order in Europe. The North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO), established in 1949 with its headquarters in Brussels, expanded to 32 members following the accession of Finland and Sweden. At the core of NATO is Article 5, which enshrines collective defense: an attack against one member is considered an attack against all. The conflict has also tested the functioning of the United Nations Security Council, where Russia holds permanent veto power under Article 27 of the UN Charter.
options: Finland
Sweden
Ukraine
Georgia
solution: Sweden formally joined NATO in 2024 as its 32nd member state.
conceptTip: NATO = 32 Members | HQ Brussels | Art 5 Defense.

topic: Supreme Court Ruling on Governor Assent to Bills
questionText: Main Idea
passageText: The constitutional role of the Governor at the state level has become a subject of intense judicial scrutiny. In a landmark case concerning bill assent delay, the Supreme Court interpreted the proviso of Article 200 of the Constitution. Under Article 200, when a Bill passed by a State Legislature is presented to the Governor, the Governor has four choices: declare assent, withhold assent, return the Bill with a message requesting reconsideration, or reserve the Bill for the President's consideration. The Court observed that if the Governor decides to withhold assent, the Governor must return the Bill 'as soon as possible' to the legislature. If the legislature passes the Bill again, with or without amendments, the Governor is constitutionally bound to give assent. The Court clarified that the Governor cannot permanently veto state legislation by simply declaring that assent is withheld without returning the Bill. This interpretation preserves the democratic structure by ensuring that the unelected constitutional head cannot overrule the elected legislature indefinitely.
options: The procedure of appointing Governors in India.
The judicial limitation placed on the Governor's power to withhold bill assent under Article 200.
The expansion of Presidential reservation powers under Article 201.
The historical origin of federalism under the Government of India Act 1935.
solution: The passage primarily discusses the Supreme Court's ruling restricting the Governor's ability to hold bills indefinitely by withholding assent without returning them.
conceptTip: 200 = Assent options | 201 = President reserve | 213 = Governor Ordinance.

Under Article 200, when a Bill passed by a State Legislature is presented to the Governor, the Governor has four choices: declare assent, withhold assent, return the Bill with a message requesting reconsideration, or reserve the Bill for the President's consideration. The Court observed that if the Governor decides to withhold assent, the Governor must return the Bill 'as soon as possible' to the legislature. If the legislature passes the Bill again, with or without amendments, the Governor is constitutionally bound to give assent. The Court clarified that the Governor cannot permanently veto state legislation by simply declaring that assent is withheld without returning the Bill. This interpretation preserves the democratic structure by ensuring that the unelected constitutional head cannot overrule the elected legislature indefinitely.
